title: Playbook or Rental? U.S. Counter-Drone Defense and the 2026 World Cup
gist: The 2026 World Cup is the first large-scale, multi-city stress test of America's brand-new (Dec 2025) state/local counter-drone authority and hastily assembled command architecture, exposing gaps in procurement maturity, operator training capacity, and inter-agency coordination that will determine whether the capability becomes a lasting national playbook or a one-off rental.

insights.claim: Until December 2025, no state or local officer in America could legally bring down a drone — that power belonged solely to federal agencies (principally DHS and DOJ) — and between 2018 and 2025 stadiums and venues asked the FBI and DHS for counter-drone help 121,000 times but received it in only 77 instances.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies just how bottlenecked the pre-2025 federal counter-drone response capacity was relative to demand — a 0.06% fulfillment rate over seven years.
insights.locator: Counter-drone authority has been a federal monopoly

insights.claim: Congress passed the SAFER SKIES Act on December 18, 2025, giving trained state and local officers counter-drone authority for the first time, but the implementing training standards and equipment rules only became an interim final rule on July 1, 2026 — mid-tournament — meaning the World Cup's opening weeks ran under a counter-drone authority whose rulebook was still being written.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A live regulatory-gap risk: the legal basis for local drone takedowns was incomplete when the highest-profile deployment of that authority began.
insights.locator: Congress acted on December 18, 2025, by passing the SAFER SKIES Act

insights.claim: Sixty state and local officers across all 11 World Cup host cities completed a rushed two-week FBI course in Alabama and were sworn in as temporary federal officers, operating on borrowed federal authority rather than the still-unfinished permanent authority.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Shows the operational workaround used to bridge the gap between the new law and its rules — a template other jurisdictions may need to replicate for future events.
insights.locator: Sixty state and local officers, spread across all 11 host cities

insights.claim: The legal authority that let local officers act was deliberately narrow — carrying federal authority only while under federal supervision, per the Justice Department's own description — and the codified permanent authority that replaced this workaround took effect July 1, mid-tournament, and has yet to be tested under real pressure; task force executive director Andrew Giuliani himself calls the interim authority a "backup plan."
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The single hardest of the three organizational tests (authorities) remains unproven at the point the tournament opened, an open legal-risk flag for a sustained multi-week deployment.

insights.claim: The 2026 World Cup spans 11 metropolitan areas over 39 days with matches running in parallel — unlike the single-city, single-day Super Bowl (run under a federal playbook nearly three decades in the making) or the 1994 US World Cup, which had no drones and used none of the same stadiums; task force executive director Andrew Giuliani described the challenge as "78 Super Bowls over 39 days."
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Establishes why this event is a genuine first-of-its-kind stress test rather than a repeat of prior playbooks — no directly comparable precedent exists.
insights.locator: The Test That Hasn't Been Run

insights.claim: No-fly zones extend 3.5 miles and rise to 3,000 feet around each World Cup venue, with smaller 1-mile/1,000-foot rings around fan festivals and separate restrictions for team hotels and training sites — creating an archipelago of closed airspace nearly 3,000 miles wide that counter-drone defenses must cover simultaneously.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies the geographic scope of the airspace-defense problem, useful for sizing the counter-drone market/coverage requirement for future national-scale events.

themes.theme: The real threat is volume, not sophistication

insights.claim: The likeliest World Cup drone threat is carelessness at volume — hobbyists, not adversaries: NFL stadiums logged about a dozen drone incursions in 2017 versus 2,845 by 2023, and DHS logged 145 incursions across eight host venues in the World Cup's opening days alone, with the FBI reporting over 500 drones seized tournament-wide, very few from malicious actors.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Sizes the true operational burden on counter-drone teams: overwhelming nuisance volume, not rare sophisticated attacks, dominates day-to-day workload.
insights.locator: The tournament's own numbers bear out the threat from so-called nuisance fliers

insights.claim: Ukraine's Operation "Spider's Web," in which small drones launched from concealed positions inside Russia struck strategic bombers far from any front line, demonstrates that distance from a conflict no longer protects a target and that the drone that reaches it need not be sophisticated — directly informing why the World Cup treats even low-tech drone threats seriously.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A precedent-setting example that reframes low-cost drones as a credible threat to any high-value target, not just front-line military assets.

insights.claim: An Iran-linked hacker group claimed to have penetrated the FBI's counter-drone feeds during the tournament (later debunked as recycled 2024 marketing footage), and separately, on June 16, federal prosecutors unsealed a complaint charging five men with plotting to fly explosive-laden drones over a White House-adjacent UFC event marking America's 250th anniversary.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Confirms the World Cup has already been publicly named as a target by an adversary and that a real explosive-drone plot against a major US event was disrupted the same week — the deliberate-threat case is not purely hypothetical.
insights.locator: That threat has already brushed the tournament

themes.theme: Battlefield tools don't transfer directly to stadiums

insights.claim: Over a packed stadium, kinetic countermeasures risk raining debris on the crowd and jamming is complicated by spectrum already saturated with friendly signals plus the fact that a jammed drone still has to land somewhere, so officials favor capturing drones intact over destroying them, working within a roughly 90-second decision window from detection.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Explains why civilian counter-drone technology choices differ fundamentally from battlefield ones — capture-oriented systems, not kinetic kill, are the preferred solution class for urban deployment.
insights.locator: On a battlefield, a drone can be shot down with minimal concern

insights.claim: The systems fielded reflect these civil constraints almost exactly: Fortem's tethered-net system, which tows a drone away intact, is the only kinetic interceptor chosen; the Sentrycs system addresses the spectrum problem by reading a drone's control link and landing it at a set spot rather than broadly jamming the airwaves; only the detection layer (radar, radio sensors, cameras) carried over largely unchanged from battlefield equipment.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Names the specific vendors selected for a major national deployment — a useful competitive-positioning data point for the counter-drone vendor landscape.
insights.locator: What was fielded aligns with those civil constraints

insights.claim: Unlike Ukraine's three-year-old, war-tested commercial drone marketplace (an "Amazon for the military" with thousands of competing systems), DHS only stood up a dedicated counter-drone purchasing office in January 2026 — five months before kickoff — leaving host cities "bombarded with vendors" without a mature procurement system to sort them.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Highlights an immature US counter-drone procurement ecosystem relative to a live-conflict benchmark — a gap that funding and market development could close ahead of future events.
insights.locator: How the buying happened is its own contrast

themes.theme: Training and command are the binding constraints

insights.claim: The FBI's National Counter-UAS Training Center graduated its first class only in December 2025 and has certified about 60 state and local officers for the entire tournament, with demand already outstripping available seats; the NFL, the 2028 Olympics, the southern border and critical infrastructure will all compete for the same limited training pipeline going forward.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies operator-training throughput, not equipment or authority, as the likely binding constraint on scaling US counter-drone capability over the next several years.
insights.locator: Its operators come through a single new schoolhouse

insights.claim: DHS itself warned in May 2026 that counter-drone systems from multiple agencies and vendors, if left uncoordinated at a single venue, "can interfere with each other and leave critical gaps in coverage" — one agency's jammer can disrupt another's first-responder network, or two agencies' sensors can blind each other — and this integration work was still unfinished when matches began.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete systems-integration risk: buying capable individual systems is not the same as operating them together, and this was unresolved at kickoff.
insights.quote: can interfere with each other and leave critical gaps in coverage
insights.locator: DHS warned in May that, left uncoordinated

insights.claim: Command runs through U.S. Northern Command (Pentagon "synchronizer"), JIATF-401, direct FBI command in Los Angeles, Miami and New York, DHS components in the other eight cities, and state/local officers under FBI supervision — a layered structure officials are actively wargaming (e.g., a January Colorado Springs tabletop exercise) to avoid a crisis where agencies are "looking around the room" asking who the decision maker is.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Maps the exact multi-agency command chain being tested — relevant for understanding decision latency and accountability if a real incident occurs.
insights.locator: Command has to be clear at both ends

themes.theme: Playbook or rental: what survives after the final whistle

insights.claim: Most of what was built for the World Cup is temporary by design: the FEMA grant that funded host cities' equipment ($250 million awarded 25 days after the application deadline, the fastest non-disaster grant program in FEMA history) runs only through September 2028 and funds no upkeep beyond it, leaving states like Missouri (with $14.2 million in equipment and certified operators) facing an open question of whether to carry the capability forward or let it lapse.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Frames a concrete post-event capital-allocation decision point (2028 funding cliff) that will determine whether World Cup counter-drone investment becomes durable national infrastructure or a one-off expenditure.
insights.locator: Some of what has been built is temporary by design

insights.claim: Some institutional pieces look built to last: the DHS purchasing office stood up in January 2026 consolidates previously scattered counter-drone buying, the Pentagon now has a standing counter-drone university mirroring the FBI's Alabama schoolhouse, and FEMA's grant program is set to reach every state and territory in its second year — aimed at a nationwide capability rather than a single event.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies which specific institutions (DHS purchasing office, Pentagon training center, FEMA's expanding grant) are likely durable legacies of the World Cup buildout, independent of the tournament itself.
insights.locator: Other pieces look built to last
